narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students' academic records, test scores, family backgrounds, and more to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try—it's designed to flag higher risks of failure to protect the school's outcomes. For you specifically, it estimated a 45% probability that you might not pass the bar on your first attempt, which crossed the threshold for denial, even though it's not a sure thing and reflects statistical patterns rather than your full potential. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2nd, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5th or 6th place standing and stood out as the most influential concern. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, which beats the average of 3.21, strongly pushed the model toward seeing you as likely to pass, acting as your second-most important strength. Your Year 1 decile of 5th, right around the average, also helped nudge things in a favorable direction, showing solid early performance. Other elements like your upper-middle family income bracket, full-time student status (the norm for most), and LSAT score of 28 played smaller roles, with your income and full-time plans offering mild boosts toward success while your LSAT had minimal impact. Compared to typical applicants, your GPA shines brighter than average, but the dip in later-year rankings raised more red flags than usual for the model. This isn't a judgment on your character, resilience, or ability to overcome barriers as a Black woman in law—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later academic deciles through additional coursework or grad school, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your unique strengths and dedication. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these areas and reapplying—your path is far from over, and with targeted improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor. Keep pushing; your story deserves to be told in the courtroom.
